root_text.sn52.10:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ āyasmā anuruddho sāvatthiyaṁ viharati andhavanasmiṁ ābādhiko dukkhito bāḷhagilāno.
root_text.sn52.10:1.2: Atha kho sambahulā bhikkhū yenāyasmā anuruddho tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā āyasmantaṁ anuruddhaṁ etadavocuṁ:
root_text.sn52.10:2.1: “Katamenāyasmato anuruddhassa vihārena viharato uppannā sārīrikā dukkhā vedanā cittaṁ na pariyādāya tiṭṭhantī”ti?
root_text.sn52.10:2.2: “Catūsu kho me, āvuso, satipaṭṭhānesu suppatiṭṭhitacittassa viharato uppannā sārīrikā dukkhā vedanā cittaṁ na pariyādāya tiṭṭhanti.
root_text.sn52.10:2.7: dhammesu dhammānupassī viharāmi ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ—
root_text.sn52.10:2.8: imesu kho me, āvuso, catūsu satipaṭṭhānesu suppatiṭṭhitacittassa viharato uppannā sārīrikā dukkhā vedanā cittaṁ na pariyādāya tiṭṭhantī”ti.

translation_text.sn52.10:1.1: At one time Venerable Anuruddha was staying near Sāvatthī in the Dark Forest. And he was sick, suffering, gravely ill.
translation_text.sn52.10:1.2: Then several mendicants went up to Venerable Anuruddha, and said to him:
translation_text.sn52.10:2.1: “What meditation does Venerable Anuruddha practice so that physical pain doesn’t occupy his mind?”
translation_text.sn52.10:2.2: “Reverends, I meditate with my mind firmly established in the four kinds of mindfulness meditation so that physical pain doesn’t occupy my mind.
translation_text.sn52.10:2.4: I meditate observing an aspect of the body …
translation_text.sn52.10:2.7: principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn52.10:2.8: I meditate with my mind firmly established in these four kinds of mindfulness meditation so that physical pain doesn’t occupy my mind.”
